nativeRange.habitat: Tropical subtidal reefs and reef slopes with strong water movement; often wedges into crevices and overhangs in hard limestone, coral or basalt substrate by day and forages over exposed rock by night.

decorPreferences: Mature live rock with established coralline and film algae for grazing
Sturdy, well-anchored rockwork that will not topple if the urchin climbs or wedges into it
Crevices and overhangs for daytime shelter
Stable, well-established saltwater system (typically 6+ months old) with low nitrate

diet.notes: Primarily a grazer on encrusting coralline algae, film algae and assorted nuisance algae (including green hair algae). Has been documented in the wild as a near-obligate coralline feeder, supplemented by Pterocladia, Ulva and similar macroalgae. In aquaria, if the rockwork does not provide enough algae the diet is generally supplemented with dried marine seaweed (nori) clipped to the rock, and some keepers offer occasional meaty bits.

goodWith: Most corals (with the rock-toppling caveat below)
avoidWith: Triggerfish and large pufferfish (will pick at spines)
Harlequin shrimp (obligate echinoderm predator, though sea-star specialists)
Unstable or lightly-stacked rockwork (urchin can dislodge frags and rocks)

summary: Indo-Pacific reef urchin with thick, blunt 'pencil' spines; widely considered one of the hardier urchin species and generally reef-safe with caution (may topple frags and graze corallines).
careNotes: Heterocentrotus mammillatus is recognised by its short, thick, blunt spines (often red, brick or dark slate) that look more like pencils or chalk sticks than the needle spines of most urchins, and is one of the more forgiving urchins in the hobby. It is generally treated as reef-safe with caution: it will happily graze coralline algae off rockwork and glass, and as a relatively large-bodied animal it can dislodge unsecured frags or topple poorly-stacked rocks as it moves, so the aquascape should be epoxied or otherwise locked together. Like all echinoderms, it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and of sudden swings in salinity or temperature; a slow drip acclimation (generally an hour or more) is advised and the urchin should not be exposed to air during transfer. Spine loss is commonly cited as an early warning sign of poor water quality, especially elevated nitrate or unstable specific gravity. Cross-source check: adult-size estimates ranged from ~10 cm (Reeflings) to ~20 cm (LiveAquaria, ~8") to one outlier 30 cm figure (ReefApp); the documented 15-20 cm body+spine span was used. Tank-size guidance also spanned 30 gal / 114 L (LiveAquaria) up to 400 L (ReefApp); the playbook's cautious-larger rounding rule was applied and the value was set to a nice 200 L (~53 US gal), consistent with Reeflings' 200 L recommendation and the species' adult footprint. Reef-safety guidance was split (LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain say 'no'; Reeflings and ReefApp say generally yes); 'with caution' was used to reflect both the frag-toppling and coralline-grazing risks. Best in a mature system (typically 6+ months) with a stable algae film for continuous grazing — starvation in algae-poor tanks is a documented failure mode.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not commonly reported in the home aquarium. In the wild, Heterocentrotus mammillatus is a broadcast spawner with an annual cycle (spawning has been reported around May-June, possibly with lunar cues) and planktonic larvae that have generally not been considered practical to rear at home.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Used as the swapped secondary source because the assigned Reef2Reef Wiki root returned HTTP 403. Cross-checked sizing (10 cm), tank size (200 L), temperature (22-27 C), pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, peaceful temperament, and habitat (Indo-Pacific reefs with strong flow).

additional.name: AquariumDomain.com
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check: confirms ~8" max, 30-gal minimum, 72-78F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12, easy care, peaceful, copper-sensitive (spine loss), and 'not reef-safe / destructive to corals' (the conservative view in the split below).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Used for the 'often reef safe' opinion in the reef-safe split, and for an upper-bound 400 L tank-size figure. Cited an outlier 30 cm adult size that was averaged out.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Heterocentrotus mamillatus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority (Echinometridae / Camarodonta / Echinoidea), Indo-Pacific distribution, 8-25 m depth range, coralline-grazing diet, and broadcast-spawning reproduction.
